Digital spy tools are changing divorce as we know it.
NPR spoke with dozens of lawyers and investigators who say more and more couples are turning to surveillance when their marriage falls apart.
From something as simple as the Find My iPhone feature to insidious spyware that can be installed in a spouse's computer or phone, these tools are cheap and easy to use.
NPR's Aarti Shahani has the story.

Well, he's a radical Sunni.
He's an extreme Islamist as well.
And he makes no apologies for his opinions.
He's supposed to be a unifying figure as a leader of the parliament.
But more often than not, he's the one responsible for making very informatory sectarian statements.
At one point last year, he said that anyone who killed Americans should be celebrated as a hero.

So it's just been one thing after another - that have been part of his statements that have finally culminated in what was really the last straw yesterday, and the parliament just declaring that it had enough of Mahmoud Mashhadani.
So he loses his post as speaker.
Will he retain his seat in parliament?
What happens next?
Yes, he will remain a member of parliament.
His Sunni bloc, the Iraqi Consensus Front, has a week to come up with a replacement.
And then the president, they're going to nominate - his nomination will be discussed with the other main blocs in parliament.
And within at the end of the week, they will vote on him.

In the meantime, his Shiite deputy is taking over and he, apparently, is better at doing the job, he's more capable, and they're hoping that he will be able to move forward with pushing the legislation that everyone is waiting for Iraq's parliament to do.
Jamie, some of the behavior attributed to Mashhadani - said to have slapped a fellow member of parliament, these kinds of shouting matches.
Tell us a little bit about parliament, the tone of the legislature there.
Some of the people that we talked to and some of the more experienced politicians say that a lot of the people in parliament are immature.
They have no experience of doing their jobs and they come out of it with a very immature perspective.
You know, parliament's supposed to meet at 11 a.m. and the press is there at 11 a.m., and most of the parliamentarians don't turn up until one.
So, you know, they don't even present themselves as being serious in their duties.
We hear a lot of repots about the absentees and that the fact that they often don't have enough numbers to hold the session.
And that in fact, when they do turn up, it's usually because the agenda of the day concerns their salaries or their benefits or things like that.
So they're not taken seriously on the streets of Baghdad.
People don't believe that the Iraqi parliamentarians actually work in their interests, and they're seen pretty much here as largely irrelevant.
And what about the question of the summer recess, a recess that could last as long as two months?
